items.subtopic: Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS
items.title: Role of von Willebrand Factor in Platelet Adhesion
items.content: Von Willebrand factor (VWF) is crucial for platelet adhesion to the site of vascular injury. It acts as a molecular glue, providing strength against shear stress and facilitating binding to subendothelial collagen.
keyPoints: VWF mediates primary platelet adhesion on denuded vascular intimal surfaces.
Provides resistance to high levels of shear stress in blood flow.
Facilitates direct binding to subendothelial collagen through specific receptors.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS

items.subtopic: Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS
items.title: Function of Gp IIb/IIIa in Platelet Aggregation
items.content: The glycoprotein (Gp) IIb/IIIa complex is essential for platelet aggregation, converting from inactive to active upon platelet activation and binding fibrinogen and VWF.
keyPoints: Gp IIb/IIIa is the most abundant receptor on the platelet surface.
Activation allows binding to fibrinogen and VWF, crucial for forming occlusive aggregates.
Each platelet has about 50,000 Gp IIb/IIIa-binding sites, enabling rapid aggregation.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS

items.title: Initiation and Amplification of Coagulation via Tissue Factor Pathway
items.content: Coagulation is initiated by tissue factor (TF) exposure following vascular damage, leading to the activation of factor VIIa. This complex activates factor X directly or indirectly through factor IXa, with critical amplification from elements of the intrinsic pathway.
keyPoints: Tissue factor (TF) initiates coagulation upon vascular injury by binding factor VIIa.
Factor X is activated to factor Xa either directly by TF/VIIa complex or indirectly via factor IXa and VIIIa.
Factor XIa functions in propagation and amplification, not initiation, of the cascade.
The role of factor XIIa in activating factor XI remains partially elucidated.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · FIBRIN CLOT FORMATION

items.title: Role of Thrombin in Fibrin Clot Formation and Stabilization
items.content: Thrombin, generated by the action of factor Xa on prothrombin, is central to converting fibrinogen into an insoluble fibrin matrix. It also activates factor XIII to stabilize the clot through cross-linking.
keyPoints: Factor Xa converts prothrombin to thrombin, a key enzyme in coagulation.
Thrombin transforms soluble plasma fibrinogen into insoluble fibrin.
Activated factor XIII (factor XIIIa) stabilizes the fibrin clot by cross-linking.
Clotting factors assemble on activated cell membranes, localizing and accelerating coagulation.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · FIBRIN CLOT FORMATION

items.title: Role of Antithrombin in Thrombosis Prevention
items.content: Antithrombin is a crucial plasma protease inhibitor that neutralizes thrombin and other activated clotting factors, preventing excessive coagulation. Its activity is significantly enhanced by heparin, which increases the rate of complex formation between antithrombin and these clotting factors.
keyPoints: Antithrombin inhibits thrombin and other clotting factors by forming complexes with their active sites.
Heparin enhances antithrombin activity by increasing the rate of inactivating complex formation by several thousand times.
Deficiencies in antithrombin lead to a predisposition to venous thromboembolism (VTE).

items.title: Mechanism of Protein C and Protein S in Anticoagulation
items.content: Protein C, when activated by thrombin on endothelial cell surfaces via thrombomodulin, acts as an anticoagulant. It requires protein S as a cofactor to enhance its efficiency in cleaving and inactivating factors V and VIII.
keyPoints: Activated protein C cleaves and inactivates activated factors V and VIII.
Protein S serves as a cofactor, enhancing the activation of protein C.
Deficiencies or resistance (e.g., factor V Leiden) to protein C or its pathway components lead to hypercoagulable states.

items.title: Understanding D-Dimer Testing for VTE Exclusion
items.content: D-dimer assays are used to measure fibrin degradation products, specifically D-dimers, which indicate the presence of blood clots. These assays serve as sensitive markers for clot formation and can be utilized to exclude deep venous thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism in selected populations.
keyPoints: D-dimer levels increase with age.
Age-adjusted D-dimer thresholds improve risk stratification, reducing unnecessary testing for VTE.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · THE FIBRINOLYTIC SYSTEM

items.title: Regulation of the Fibrinolytic System
items.content: The fibrinolytic system is regulated at three primary levels: plasminogen activator inhibitors (PAIs), thrombin-activatable fibrinolysis inhibitor (TAFI), and alpha-2 antiplasmin. PAI-1 primarily inhibits tissue-type and urokinase-type plasminogen activators, TAFI limits fibrinolysis by modifying fibrin structure, and alpha-2 antiplasmin inactivates free plasmin.
keyPoints: PAI-1 is the main inhibitor of tPA and uPA in plasma.
TAFI cleaves N-terminal lysine residues on fibrin to localize plasmin activity.
Alpha-2 antiplasmin primarily inhibits nonfibrin clot-associated plasmin.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · THE FIBRINOLYTIC SYSTEM

items.title: Bleeding Score as a Tool to Predict Inherited Bleeding Disorders
items.content: The bleeding score is validated for predicting inherited bleeding disorders, particularly type 1 von Willebrand disease (VWD). It helps in excluding the diagnosis of a bleeding disorder and avoids unnecessary testing. The tool is recommended by 2021 guidelines for screening VWD in primary care.
keyPoints: Bleeding score predicts patients more likely to have an inherited bleeding disorder, especially type 1 VWD.
Self-administered form validated for use.
Recommended by 2021 guidelines for screening VWD in primary care.

items.title: Characteristics of Heavy Menstrual Bleeding
items.content: Heavy menstrual bleeding is defined as a loss of more than 80 mL of blood per cycle. It often correlates with iron-deficiency anemia and may require medical intervention such as transfusions. Predictors include passing clots larger than 1 inch, changing pads or tampons hourly, and starting at menarche.
keyPoints: Defined as a loss of >80 mL of blood per cycle.
Correlates with iron-deficiency anemia.
Predictors: clots >1 inch, pad/tampon change >hourly.

items.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage in Women with Bleeding Disorders
items.content: Women with bleeding disorders like type 1 VWD or hemophilia A may experience postpartum hemorrhage, often delayed due to normalization of VWF and factor VIII levels during pregnancy. There is a higher risk of recurrence with subsequent pregnancies.
keyPoints: Postpartum hemorrhage common in women with bleeding disorders.
Delayed hemorrhage possible if VWF/factor VIII normalize during pregnancy.
Higher risk of recurrence in subsequent pregnancies.

items.title: Timing and Risk of Postoperative Bleeding
items.content: Bleeding after tonsillectomy can occur early or approximately 7 days postoperatively, coinciding with the loss of eschar. Similar delayed bleeding is observed following colonic polyp resection.
keyPoints: Early or delayed postoperative bleeding may occur in tonsillectomy and colonic polypectomy
Loss of eschar at 7 days post-tonsillectomy can trigger bleeding

items.title: Bleeding Risks with Medications and Supplements
items.content: Aspirin and NSAIDs impair primary hemostasis, potentially exacerbating or unmasking bleeding disorders like VWD. Herbal supplements such as fish oil can also impair platelet function.
keyPoints: Aspirin affects platelet function for the life of the platelet; functional defect reverts near-normal within 2–3 days after cessation
NSAIDs and ADP P2Y12 receptor inhibitors increase bleeding risk, especially in those with underlying disorders
Fish oil supplements alter platelet biochemistry, potentially increasing bleeding time

items.title: Bleeding Tendencies Associated with Systemic Diseases and Thrombocytopenia
items.content: Systemic diseases like liver disease, renal impairment, and bone marrow failure can cause or exacerbate bleeding tendencies. Thrombocytopenia rarely causes bleeding at counts >50,000/μL.
keyPoints: Liver disease, severe renal impairment, hypothyroidism, paraproteinemias, amyloidosis, and bone marrow failure can present with bruising or mucosal bleeding
Normal platelet count: 150,000–450,000/μL; significant bleeding risk usually not until <10,000–20,000/μL in thrombocytopenia

items.title: Idiopathic Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) as a Predictor of Recurrence
items.content: In patients without underlying malignancy, an idiopathic venous thrombotic event is the strongest predictor for recurrence. This highlights the importance of identifying whether a VTE was idiopathic or precipitated during patient history assessment.
keyPoints: Idiopathic events are strong predictors of VTE recurrence in non-malignant patients.
Assessing if a thrombotic event is idiopathic helps predict future risk.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · HISTORY OF THROMBOSIS

items.title: Age and Incidence of Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT)
items.content: The incidence of DVT increases with age, from approximately 1/100,000 per year in early childhood to about 1/200 per year among octogenarians. This underscores the importance of considering age as a significant risk factor when evaluating patients for venous thrombosis.
keyPoints: DVT incidence rises with age: ~1/100,000/year in children to ~1/200/year in octogenarians.
Age is a critical factor in assessing DVT risk.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · HISTORY OF THROMBOSIS

items.title: Understanding the Limitations of PT and aPTT in Bleeding Risk Assessment
items.content: The prothrombin time (PT) is useful for detecting liver disease or vitamin K deficiency, while the activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) lacks confirmed usefulness in preoperative evaluations for patients with no bleeding history. Both tests have limitations and should not be used as sole indicators of bleeding risk.
keyPoints: No test provides a global assessment of hemostasis.
Bleeding time is not recommended for predicting bleeding risk.
PT may detect liver disease or vitamin K deficiency.
aPTT lacks confirmed usefulness in preoperative evaluations without a bleeding history.
Coagulation tests should confirm the presence and type of bleeding disorder with suspicious clinical history.

items.title: Critical Factors in Coagulation Assay Validity
items.content: Proper sample acquisition and handling are crucial for valid coagulation assay results. Repeat studies with attention to these factors can normalize values in patients without a bleeding history but with abnormal initial assays.
keyPoints: Sample acquisition and handling are critical for valid results.
Repeat studies often normalize values if no bleeding history is present.
Vacutainer tubes should be filled >90% of the recommended fill.
Elevated hematocrit (>55%) can cause false assay values.

items.title: Interpreting Coagulation Test Abnormalities
items.content: Coagulation test abnormalities correlate with specific hemostatic disorders. Prolonged aPTT and PT can indicate various deficiencies or anticoagulant effects, but the degree of prolongation does not precisely predict bleeding risk.
keyPoints: Prolonged aPTT may indicate factor XII, XI, VIII, IX deficiencies, or heparin use.
Prolonged PT suggests factor VII deficiency, vitamin K deficiency, warfarin use, or direct Xa inhibitors.
Both prolonged aPTT and PT can indicate deficiencies in factors II, V, X, fibrinogen, or the presence of direct thrombin inhibitors.
The degree of prolongation does not precisely predict bleeding risk.

items.title: Interpreting Prolonged PT/aPTT with Mixing Studies
items.content: Mixing studies help differentiate between factor deficiencies and inhibitors when prolonged aPTT or PT is observed. A correction in clotting time after mixing indicates a factor deficiency, whereas no correction suggests the presence of an inhibitor.
keyPoints: Isolated factor deficiencies correct with mixing and stay corrected upon incubation.
Lupus anticoagulant shows no correction with mixing or incubation.
Acquired factor VIII inhibitors may not correct immediately but prolong with incubation.

items.title: Assessing Lupus Anticoagulants
items.content: Lupus anticoagulants are detected using assays like LA-PTT and dRVVT, which increase sensitivity to phospholipid-dependent coagulation interference. Confirmation requires lack of correction with normal plasma mixing and correction with activated platelet membranes.
keyPoints: LA-PTT uses a sensitive reagent for lupus anticoagulant detection.
dRVVT increases sensitivity by decreasing the phospholipid reagent.
Confirmation involves lack of correction with normal plasma and correction with specific phospholipids.

items.title: Thrombin Time in Direct Thrombin Inhibitor Monitoring
items.content: The thrombin time is used to assess the activity of direct thrombin inhibitors like dabigatran. It becomes markedly prolonged in their presence, and a dilute thrombin time can quantify drug activity.
keyPoints: Thrombin time is prolonged with direct thrombin inhibitors such as dabigatran.
A dilute thrombin time assesses the activity of these drugs.

items.title: Timing of Thrombophilia Testing
items.content: Thrombophilia evaluations should ideally be conducted in a steady state, away from acute events. This is crucial because various factors such as acute thrombosis and medications can alter the levels of coagulation factors and their inhibitors.
keyPoints: Testing should be performed remote from the acute event to ensure accuracy.
Acute thrombosis decreases antithrombin and increases Protein C and S levels.
Warfarin decreases Protein C and S levels.
Functional assays are affected by anticoagulants like warfarin, thrombin, and Xa inhibitors.
Testing can be performed after stopping drugs for at least 3 weeks post the initial 3–6 months of treatment.

items.title: Indications for Genetic Thrombophilia Testing
items.content: Genetic thrombophilia testing should be reserved for cases with a strong family history of thrombosis where the results would influence clinical decision-making. This is because genetic assays are not affected by anticoagulants, unlike functional assays.
keyPoints: Testing for genetic thrombophilias should only occur if there's a strong family history of thrombosis.
Results must be actionable in terms of affecting clinical decisions.
Genetic assays remain unaffected by anticoagulant medications.

items.subtopic: Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS
items.question: Which protein primarily mediates platelet adhesion to the site of vascular injury?
items.explanation: Von Willebrand factor serves as the primary 'molecular glue' for platelet adhesion.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS

items.subtopic: Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS
items.question: What is the role of the glycoprotein IIb/IIIa complex during platelet activation?
options: It binds to fibrinogen and VWF, facilitating platelet aggregation.
It releases anticoagulant factors from endothelial cells.
It degrades collagen in the vessel wall.
items.explanation: The glycoprotein IIb/IIIa complex binds to fibrinogen and VWF, enabling platelet aggregation.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS

items.subtopic: Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT directly involved in enhancing platelet activation and aggregation?
items.explanation: Fibrinogen is not a mediator that enhances platelet activation; it serves as a bridge for platelet aggregation.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS

items.question: Which pathway is primarily responsible for initiating blood coagulation?
items.explanation: Coagulation is normally initiated through tissue factor (TF) exposure and activation via the extrinsic pathway.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · FIBRIN CLOT FORMATION

items.question: What role does thrombin play in fibrin clot formation?
options: Thrombin converts prothrombin to factor Xa.
Thrombin activates factor XIII, which stabilizes the fibrin clot.
Thrombin directly forms insoluble fibrin from soluble plasma fibrinogen.
items.explanation: Thrombin converts soluble plasma fibrinogen to an insoluble fibrin matrix.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · FIBRIN CLOT FORMATION

items.question: Which factor is activated by thrombin-induced limited proteolysis of its precursor?
items.explanation: The essential cofactor for the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin is factor Va, produced by thrombin-induced limited proteolysis of factor V.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · FIBRIN CLOT FORMATION

items.question: Which molecule, when activated by thrombin on endothelial cells, acts as an anticoagulant by inactivating factors V and VIII?
items.explanation: Activated protein C, when activated by thrombin on endothelial cells via thrombomodulin, inactivates factors V and VIII.

items.question: What is the primary role of antithrombin in the coagulation process?
options: Neutralizing thrombin and other clotting factors
items.explanation: Antithrombin neutralizes thrombin and other activated coagulation factors by forming complexes with them.

items.question: Which factor is responsible for the anticoagulant effects of heparin in enhancing the activity of antithrombin?
items.explanation: Heparin enhances the activity of antithrombin by interacting with glycosaminoglycans, which catalyze the inactivation of thrombin and other clotting factors.

items.question: Which enzyme is primarily responsible for cleaving the Arg560-Val561 bond of plasminogen to generate active plasmin?
items.explanation: Both tPA and uPA cleave the Arg560-Val561 bond of plasminogen to generate active plasmin.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · THE FIBRINOLYTIC SYSTEM

items.question: What is a specific test used to measure fibrin degradation in plasma?
items.explanation: D-dimer assays are used as specific tests to measure fibrin degradation in plasma.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · THE FIBRINOLYTIC SYSTEM

items.question: Which inhibitor primarily inhibits tissue type plasminogen activator (tPA) and urokinase-type plasminogen activator (uPA) in plasma?
items.explanation: PAI-1 is the primary inhibitor of tPA and uPA in plasma.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · THE FIBRINOLYTIC SYSTEM

items.question: Which inherited disorder is most commonly associated with spontaneous hemarthroses?
options: Moderate and severe factor VIII and IX deficiency
items.explanation: Spontaneous hemarthroses are a hallmark of moderate and severe factor VIII and IX deficiency.

items.question: What is the most useful tool recommended by 2021 guidelines for screening for von Willebrand disease in primary care?
items.explanation: A bleeding score has been validated as a tool to predict patients more likely to have an inherited bleeding disorder, particularly type 1 VWD.

items.question: What is the quantitative definition of heavy menstrual bleeding based on blood loss per cycle?
items.explanation: Heavy menstrual bleeding is defined quantitatively as a loss of >80 mL of blood per cycle.

items.question: Which condition might cause easy bruising without an identifiable coagulopathy?
items.explanation: Easy bruising can be a sign of conditions like Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, where there is no identifiable coagulopathy.

items.question: Which factor deficiency is most commonly associated with hemarthroses and spontaneous muscle hematomas?
items.explanation: Hemarthroses and spontaneous muscle hematomas are characteristic of moderate or severe congenital factor VIII or IX deficiency.

items.question: What is the typical time frame for delayed bleeding after a tonsillectomy?
items.explanation: Delayed bleeding after a tonsillectomy typically occurs approximately 7 days postoperatively.

items.question: Which medication class can unmask a previously occult mild bleeding disorder such as VWD?
items.explanation: Aspirin and other NSAIDs can unmask a previously occult mild bleeding disorder such as VWD.

items.question: What is the primary cause of bleeding-related deaths in patients with severe congenital factor deficiencies?
items.explanation: Central nervous system bleeding is the major cause of bleeding-related deaths in patients with severe congenital factor deficiencies.

items.question: Which factor is the major risk factor for arterial thrombosis?
items.explanation: The passage states that atherosclerosis is the major risk factor for arterial thrombosis.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · HISTORY OF THROMBOSIS

items.question: In patients without underlying malignancy, what is the strongest predictor of recurrence of venous thromboembolism (VTE)?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that having an idiopathic event is the strongest predictor of recurrence of VTE in patients without underlying malignancy.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · HISTORY OF THROMBOSIS

items.question: What should testing for inherited thrombophilias in adults be limited to?
options: Instances where results would change clinical care
items.explanation: The passage mentions that testing for inherited thrombophilias should be limited to instances where results would change clinical care.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · HISTORY OF THROMBOSIS

items.question: Which laboratory test is commonly used to assess the vitamin K-dependent clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X?
items.explanation: The Prothrombin Time (PT) assesses the vitamin K-dependent clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X.

items.question: What is a limitation of using PT to predict bleeding risk in patients with liver disease?
options: PT overestimates the bleeding risk due to its sensitivity to direct Xa inhibitors.
PT does not measure all aspects of hemostasis affected by liver dysfunction, potentially overestimating bleeding risk.
PT results are highly variable between laboratories.
PT is insensitive to vitamin K deficiency.
items.explanation: The PT only measures one aspect of hemostasis affected by liver dysfunction and may overestimate the bleeding risk.

items.question: Which factor is NOT assessed by the aPTT?
items.explanation: The aPTT assesses factors in the intrinsic and common pathways, but not Factor VII.

items.question: What is the primary use of coagulation testing according to clinical history?
options: To screen for all possible bleeding disorders in asymptomatic patients.
To confirm the presence and type of bleeding disorder in a patient with a suspicious clinical history.
To assess thrombin generation across different laboratories.
items.explanation: The primary use is to confirm the presence and type of bleeding disorder in patients with a suspicious clinical history.

items.question: Which test is specifically prolonged by the presence of direct thrombin inhibitor, dabigatran?
items.explanation: The thrombin time is markedly prolonged in the presence of the direct thrombin inhibitor, dabigatran.

items.question: What does a lack of correction after mixing studies with normal plasma indicate?
items.explanation: A lack of correction after mixing studies indicates the presence of an inhibitor.

items.question: Which test is used to assess activity levels of low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) and direct Xa inhibitors?
items.explanation: Measurement of anti-factor Xa plasma inhibitory activity is used to assess LMWH levels and the activity of direct Xa inhibitors.

items.question: What does a prolonged thrombin time but not reptilase time suggest?
items.explanation: The thrombin time, but not the reptilase time, is prolonged in the presence of heparin.

items.question: When should laboratory evaluation for thrombophilia ideally be conducted to ensure accurate results?
options: In a steady state, remote from the acute event
While on heparin or other anticoagulants
items.explanation: Testing should be performed in a steady state, remote from the acute event to ensure accurate results.

items.question: Which laboratory assays are affected by anticoagulants such as warfarin and thrombin inhibitors?
items.explanation: Functional assays are affected by anticoagulants, including warfarin and thrombin inhibitors.

items.question: Under what condition should testing for genetic thrombophilias generally be performed?
options: In all patients with a history of thrombosis
Only when there is a strong family history and results would affect clinical decision-making
During pregnancy regardless of family history
Whenever anticoagulation therapy is being considered
items.explanation: Testing for genetic thrombophilias should generally be performed only with a strong family history and if the results would impact clinical decisions.

items.subtopic: Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS
items.statement: Platelet adhesion is mediated primarily by direct binding to subendothelial collagen.
items.explanation: Von Willebrand factor, not direct binding to collagen, primarily mediates platelet adhesion.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS

items.subtopic: Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS
items.statement: Activated platelets release contents that promote aggregation and inhibit anticoagulant endothelial cell factors.
items.explanation: During the release reaction, activated platelets secrete substances that enhance aggregation and counteract anticoagulants.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS

items.statement: Factor XI activation in hemostasis depends on its activation by factor XIIa.
items.explanation: The participation of factor XI is dependent on positive feedback activation by thrombin, not directly by factor XIIa.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · FIBRIN CLOT FORMATION

items.statement: Activated platelet surfaces provide phospholipid support for coagulation reactions.
items.explanation: Coagulation reactions take place on phospholipid surfaces, usually the activated platelet surface.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · FIBRIN CLOT FORMATION

items.statement: Antithrombin deficiency leads to a decreased risk of venous thromboembolism.
items.explanation: Deficiencies in antithrombin lead to an increased risk of venous thromboembolism due to reduced inhibition of thrombin and other clotting factors.

items.statement: Protein C activation is enhanced by its binding to endothelial cell receptors.
items.explanation: Binding of protein C to its receptor on endothelial cells enhances its activation efficiency in the presence of thrombin-thrombomodulin complex.

items.statement: D-dimer levels decrease with age.
items.explanation: D-dimer levels increase with age.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · THE FIBRINOLYTIC SYSTEM

items.statement: TAFI limits fibrinolysis by cleaving the N-terminal lysine residues of fibrin.
items.explanation: TAFI limits fibrinolysis by cleaving the N-terminal lysine residues of fibrin.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · THE FIBRINOLYTIC SYSTEM

items.statement: Epistaxis in children and dry climates always indicates an underlying bleeding disorder.
items.explanation: Epistaxis is common, especially in children and dry climates, but does not always reflect an underlying bleeding disorder.

items.statement: Postpartum hemorrhage may be delayed in women with type 1 VWD or hemophilia A.
items.explanation: In these conditions, levels of VWF and factor VIII often normalize during pregnancy, potentially delaying postpartum hemorrhage.

items.statement: Bleeding into a joint often results in discoloration from bruising around the joint.
items.explanation: Bleeding into a joint results in severe pain and swelling, but is rarely associated with discoloration from bruising.

items.statement: Thrombocytopenia usually causes bleeding when platelet counts are below 50,000/μL.
items.explanation: Bleeding rarely occurs in isolated thrombocytopenia at counts >50,000/μL and usually not until <10,000–20,000/μL.

items.statement: Age is not an important risk factor for venous thrombosis.
items.explanation: The passage states that age is an important risk factor, with the risk of DVT increasing per decade.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · HISTORY OF THROMBOSIS

items.statement: A genetic thrombophilia like prothrombin G20210A mutation significantly increases the risk of thrombosis in elderly individuals undergoing high-risk surgical procedures.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that such a genetic thrombophilia is a minor determinant of risk in these circumstances.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · HISTORY OF THROMBOSIS

items.statement: Thrombin generation assays provide reproducible results across laboratories.
items.explanation: Thrombin generation assays have not generally provided reproducible results across laboratories.

items.statement: The bleeding time is recommended for predicting bleeding risk.
items.explanation: The bleeding time does not predict bleeding risk and is not recommended for this indication.

items.statement: A dilute Russell viper venom test (dRVVT) increases sensitivity to factor deficiencies.
items.explanation: The dRVVT is modified to increase sensitivity to antibodies that interfere with the phospholipid component, not factor deficiencies.

items.statement: Mixing studies can distinguish between a factor deficiency and an inhibitor.
items.explanation: Mixing studies are used to differentiate between a factor deficiency and the presence of an inhibitor.

items.statement: Antithrombin levels are decreased in the setting of acute thrombosis.
items.explanation: Antithrombin is indeed decreased by heparin and in the setting of acute thrombosis.

items.statement: Protein C and S levels are increased during warfarin therapy.
items.explanation: Protein C and S levels are decreased by warfarin, not increased.

items.subtopic: Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS
items.assertion: The glycoprotein IIb/IIIa complex is crucial for the formation of an occlusive platelet thrombus.
items.reason: It enables binding to fibrinogen and VWF, allowing numerous activated platelets to form a dense network.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the glycoprotein IIb/IIIa complex is crucial.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · Bleeding and Thrombosis | STEPS OF NORMAL HEMOSTASIS

items.assertion: Protein S is essential for the anticoagulant activity of activated protein C.
items.reason: Activated protein C requires protein S as a cofactor to accelerate its cleavage and inactivation of factors V and VIII.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why protein S is essential for activated protein C's function.

items.assertion: The primary function of alpha2-antiplasmin is to inhibit plasmin.
items.reason: Alpha2-antiplasmin inactivates any nonfibrin clot-associated plasmin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · THE FIBRINOLYTIC SYSTEM

items.assertion: Heavy menstrual bleeding is a common symptom in women with von Willebrand disease.
items.reason: Women with underlying bleeding disorders are more likely to have other bleeding symptoms, including heavy menstrual bleeding.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why heavy menstrual bleeding is common in women with von Willebrand disease.

items.assertion: Easy bruising is always indicative of a bleeding disorder.
items.reason: Easy bruising can also result from conditions like Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, where there's no identifiable coagulopathy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because easy bruising can occur without an identifiable coagulopathy.

items.assertion: VWD is associated with angiodysplasia of the bowel and GI bleeding.
items.reason: VWD, particularly types 2 and 3, affects hemostatic mechanisms leading to these complications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.

items.assertion: NSAIDs can cause GI bleeding in patients with underlying bleeding disorders.
items.reason: The aspirin effect on platelet function lasts for the life of the platelet, increasing bleeding risk.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not explain why NSAIDs can cause GI bleeding in patients with underlying disorders.

items.assertion: A history of being treated with warfarin or other anticoagulants suggests a past deep vein thrombosis (DVT).
items.reason: Patients with vague histories of thrombosis who have been treated with anticoagulants likely had DVT.
items.explanation: The passage supports both the assertion and reason, indicating that a history of anticoagulant treatment suggests past DVT.
items.reference: Bleeding and Thrombosis · HISTORY OF THROMBOSIS

items.assertion: Proper sample acquisition and handling are critical to obtaining valid coagulation assay results.
items.reason: In patients with abnormal coagulation assays but no bleeding history, repeat studies often result in normal values when proper sample handling is ensured.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why proper sample acquisition and handling are critical.

items.assertion: The aPTT is useful for evaluating patients with liver disease.
items.reason: Thrombin generation has been shown to be normal in many patients with mild to moderate liver dysfunction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the passage does not state that aPTT is specifically useful for liver disease evaluation; the reason is true but unrelated.

items.assertion: The aPTT reagent's sensitivity varies with individual factor deficiencies.
items.reason: With isolated factor deficiencies, the aPTT will correct with mixing and stay corrected with incubation.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the explanation for the assertion.

items.assertion: The dRVVT test is specific for lupus anticoagulants.
items.reason: Factor deficiencies or other inhibitors can also result in prolongation of the dRVVT test.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the dRVVT test is not specific for lupus anticoagulants.

items.assertion: Testing for antiphospholipid antibodies should be avoided during acute illness.
items.reason: Antiphospholipid antibodies are frequently transiently positive in acute illness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why testing should be avoided.
